title: Meet Python
subtitle: Say hello to the friendliest programming language on Earth.
goal: Write and run your very first Python program, and understand what print() and comments do.

blocks.heading: What is Python? 🐍
blocks.html:
Python is a way of giving a computer instructions using words that look almost like English. People use it to build games, websites, robots, and even the apps on your phone.

The best part? You don't need to install anything today. Every code box on this page runs real Python right inside your browser. You type, you press Run, the snake does the work. 🎉

blocks.heading: Your first command: print()
blocks.html:
The word print tells Python: “show this on the screen.” Whatever you put inside the quotes is what shows up.

Press ▶ Run on the box below and watch what happens.

blocks.caption: Your first Python program
blocks.explain: print() shows whatever is inside the parentheses. The quotes "..." tell Python this is text (we call text a 'string').

blocks.html: Quotes matter! Text always goes inside quotes: "like this". Forget them and Python gets confused.

blocks.caption: You can print more than once
blocks.code:
print("I am learning Python.")
print("This is line two!")
print("🐍 is fun.")
blocks.explain: Each print() makes its own new line. Python runs your lines from top to bottom, in order.

blocks.heading: Comments: notes for humans 📝
blocks.html: Anything after a # is a comment. Python completely ignores it — comments are little notes for you and other people, not for the computer.

blocks.caption: The # parts are ignored by Python
blocks.code:
# This is a note to myself. Python skips it.
print("Python ignores the line above")  # you can comment at the end too
blocks.explain: Comments help you remember WHY you wrote something. Good coders leave helpful notes.

blocks.question: What does print("Hi") do?
options: Prints the word Hi on the screen
blocks.explain: print() shows whatever is inside the quotes — here, the text Hi.

blocks.question: What happens to text after a # symbol?
blocks.explain: The # starts a comment. Python skips everything after it on that line.

blocks.title: Print your own name
blocks.instructions: Change the text inside the quotes so the program prints YOUR name. Then press Check.
blocks.passMsg: Awesome — you just made the computer talk!
blocks.failMsg: Make sure you keep print("...") and put your name inside the quotes.

blocks.title: Three facts about you
blocks.instructions: Use three print() lines to show three fun facts about yourself (your age, favorite food, favorite color — anything!).
blocks.starter:
print("Fact 1: ...")
# add two more print lines below
blocks.solution:
print("Fact 1: I am 12 years old")
print("Fact 2: I love pizza")
print("Fact 3: My favorite color is blue")
blocks.passMsg: Three lines, three facts — nicely done!
blocks.failMsg: Add at least three print() lines, each on its own line.

challenge.title: Make an ASCII greeting card
challenge.instructions: Use print() lines to draw a little greeting on screen. Print a top border, a message in the middle, and a bottom border. Make it say hello to someone you like!
challenge.starter:
print("*****************")
# print a greeting line in the middle
print("*****************")
challenge.hint: You need at least three print() lines: a border, your message, and another border.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 Beautiful card! You've finished Day 1 — you're officially a programmer now.
